2021 · ICLR 2022 · Post-training & RL

Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

The paper that named instruction tuning: finetune a 137B pretrained LM on 60+ existing NLP datasets rephrased as natural-language instructions, and it learns to follow instructions for task types it never saw in tuning. Zero-shot FLAN beats zero-shot GPT-3 175B on 20 of 25 datasets — but the benefit only emerges above ~8B parameters.

Jason Wei*, Maarten Bosma*, Vincent Y. Zhao*, Kelvin Guu*, Adams Wei Yu, Brian Lester, Nan Du, Andrew M. Dai, Quoc V. Le — Google Research
venue ICLR 2022 citations 5,219 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

GPT-3 made the case that language models at scale are remarkable few-shot learners — give them a handful of in-context exemplars and they generalize. But the same models are conspicuously bad at zero-shot: on reading comprehension, question answering, and natural language inference, GPT-3's zero-shot numbers trail its few-shot numbers by a wide margin. The instinctive fix — write a cleverer prompt — is brittle and dataset-specific.

Why is zero-shot so much harder? The authors' diagnosis is about format, not knowledge. Without exemplars, a raw LM is handed a prompt that looks nothing like the continuous web text it was pretrained on; it has no signal that it is even being asked to "perform a task." An NLI prompt, in particular, is "unlikely to have appeared naturally in an unsupervised training set."

Prior recipes each gave up something. Pretrain–finetune (BERT, T5) yields a strong specialist but needs labeled data per task and produces one model per task. Prompting (GPT-3) keeps one general model but leans on exemplars or prompt engineering. The gap FLAN targets: can a single pretrained model be taught the general behavior of "read a plain-English instruction and do the task," so that it transfers to instructions for tasks it never saw during training — with no exemplars at all?

// how it works

The method

Instruction tuning is deliberately simple: take a pretrained LM and finetune it on a large mixture of existing NLP datasets, but with every example reverbalized as a natural-language instruction. The base model is LaMDA-PT, a dense, left-to-right, decoder-only transformer with 137B parameters, pretrained on 2.49T BPE tokens of web text (including code), dialog, and Wikipedia — roughly 10% non-English. (This is the language-model-only checkpoint, not the dialog-finetuned LaMDA.) The instruction-tuned result is called FLAN — Finetuned Language Net.

The tuning corpus is built from 62 publicly available text datasets, hand-sorted into 12 task clusters by task type: natural language inference, reading comprehension, closed-book QA, commonsense, sentiment, paraphrase, coreference, translation (8 language pairs), struct-to-text, summarization, reading-comprehension-with-commonsense, and a miscellaneous cluster. For each dataset the authors manually wrote 10 instruction templates; to add diversity, up to three of the ten "turn the task around" (e.g., for sentiment, ask the model to generate a movie review of a given polarity). At training time each example is wrapped in a randomly chosen template for its dataset.

Training is a standard supervised fine-tune — ordinary LM cross-entropy on the target, with the input wrapped in its template:

FLAN = argmaxθ  𝔼 d∼D, t∼Td, (x,y)∼d  log pθ( y | t(x) ) // for dataset d, sample a template t and an example (x,y); maximize the likelihood of the target y given the instruction-wrapped input t(x)

Concrete recipe: cap each dataset at 30k examples, mix via T5-style examples-proportional sampling (mixing-rate max 3k), 30k gradient steps, batch of 8,192 tokens, Adafactor at learning rate 3e-5, input/target lengths 1024/256 with packing. The whole instruction tune is ~60 hours on a 128-core TPUv3 — less than 2% of the compute that pretrained the base model. For classification tasks, an OPTIONS suffix lists the allowed answers so the probability mass isn't split across synonymous phrasings.

The load-bearing design choice is the evaluation split. "Unseen" is defined at the cluster level, not the dataset level: a task counts as unseen only if no dataset from its entire cluster appeared in tuning. So to report zero-shot NLI, the authors instruction-tune a model on every cluster except NLI, then evaluate it on NLI. Reporting zero-shot numbers for c clusters therefore means tuning c different checkpoints, each holding out a different cluster — a conservative protocol that rules out same-task-type leakage.

// what they found

The results

With the best dev-set template, zero-shot FLAN beats zero-shot GPT-3 175B on 20 of 25 datasets, and on 10 of them it even surpasses GPT-3's few-shot performance — including by a large margin on ANLI, RTE, BoolQ, AI2-ARC, OpenBookQA, and StoryCloze. Against the sparse, mixture-of-experts GLaM 64B/64E, zero-shot FLAN wins on 13 of 19 datasets and beats one-shot GLaM on 11 of 19. The headline averages on the three task types where instruction tuning helped most:

Unseen task typeGPT-3 175B
0-shot		GPT-3 175B
few-shot		FLAN 137B
0-shot
Natural language inference (5)42.953.256.2
Reading comprehension (3)63.772.677.4
Closed-book QA (4)49.855.756.6

The cleanest comparison is the one within the same model. LaMDA-PT 137B — the identical checkpoint before instruction tuning, prompted exactly as GPT-3 — is the direct ablation, and instruction tuning improves it significantly on most datasets. The GPT-3 and GLaM bars are different models with different pretraining data; the LaMDA-PT delta is the part that isolates instruction tuning itself.

It does not help everywhere. On tasks that are already formatted as plain language modeling — commonsense reasoning and coreference posed as sentence completion — instructions are redundant, and FLAN beats LaMDA-PT on only 3 of 7 such tasks. Translation is asymmetric: FLAN beats zero-shot GPT-3 on all six evaluations and rivals supervised systems when translating into English, but is markedly weaker translating out of English, as expected from an English tokenizer and overwhelmingly English pretraining.

The ablations are where the real lessons are

More clusters help, monotonically. Sweeping from 1 to 7 instruction-tuning clusters, average accuracy on three held-out clusters climbs from 49.9 → 63.5 and shows no sign of saturating — implying still more task variety would help.

Instructions matter, not just multitask data. Strip the instructions out and the effect collapses. Finetuning on the same datasets labeled only by their dataset name scores 47.0; with no template at all, 37.3; FLAN's natural instructions reach 55.2 (4-cluster average). So the gain is not generic multi-task finetuning — it is specifically learning to follow instructions.

Few-shot and prompt tuning are complementary. Adding up to 16 in-context exemplars improves every cluster (most for struct-to-text, translation, and closed-book QA) and lowers template-to-template variance. And FLAN is a better starting point for prompt tuning than LaMDA-PT, with >10% gains in the low-resource (32-example) setting.

But the most-cited finding is the scaling curve.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The central claim — instruction tuning makes a large LM follow instructions for task types it never saw — is well evidenced, and unusually, the strongest evidence is not the headline. The "20 of 25 vs GPT-3" line is the marketing number: GPT-3 (175B) and GLaM (64B sparse) differ from FLAN in size and pretraining data, so that comparison conflates instruction tuning with everything else. The science is the LaMDA-PT control — the same 137B weights, the same GPT-3-style prompts, minus the instruction tune — and FLAN beats it on most datasets. To the authors' credit, they foreground that ablation rather than hide it.

Two things deflate the headline if you read closely. First, the 20/25 uses the best of 10 templates selected on a dev set; the paper also reports the mean over templates (the "typical instruction" proxy), which is lower, and template-to-template standard deviation is non-trivial (visible in the few-shot figure). A practitioner who writes one prompt gets the mean, not the max. Second, "unseen" is conservative at the cluster level — a genuine strength — but all 62 datasets are standard academic benchmarks. FLAN generalizes across NLP task types; it is not yet the open-ended, follow-any-user-request behavior the word "instruction" later came to mean (that is InstructGPT's contribution). And mechanically, each zero-shot number comes from a different held-out checkpoint — there is no single downloadable "FLAN" that legitimately produces all of them at once.

The scale gate is the honest limitation the paper is most remembered for: the effect appears only at ≥68B, and at ≤8B instruction tuning actively hurts held-out tasks. The capacity-saturation story is a hypothesis, not a tested mechanism — and it turned out to be recipe-specific: concurrent T0 (11B) and the later FLAN-T5 work showed instruction tuning helps far smaller models with better task mixtures, so "needs ~100B" was a property of this particular recipe, not of instruction tuning itself.

baselines

Cross-model bars (GPT-3 175B, GLaM 64B) are confounded by size and pretraining data. The clean control is LaMDA-PT — same 137B weights, same prompts, no tuning — and FLAN beats it on most datasets. Trust that within-model delta over the 20/25-vs-GPT-3 headline; the authors foreground it.

contamination

They replicate GPT-3's n-gram clean/dirty analysis and find no correlation that overlap inflated scores. DROP, SQuADv2, and ANLI R1/R2 were nearly fully contaminated, but in passage contexts, not in answers (e.g. answer overlap in only 5 of 11,153 SQuADv2 examples). Honest for 2021, with the conservative-n-gram and same-distribution caveats acknowledged.

what's missed

Headline leans on dev-selected best-of-10 templates; the per-prompt user gets the lower mean with real template variance. "Unseen" means an unseen academic task cluster, not arbitrary instructions. Each reported number is a separate held-out model, not one artifact.

would raise confidence

Directly test the capacity-saturation hypothesis rather than assert it; evaluate on instruction types outside academic benchmarks; surface template variance in the main results, not the appendix; report a single all-but-one held-out audit end to end.

// bottom line

FLAN's claim holds up where it counts: the within-model LaMDA-PT ablation is decisive, the role-of-instructions ablation (55.2 vs 47.0 vs 37.3) cleanly attributes the gain to instructions, and the contamination check is candid. The fragilities are scope, not validity — the effect needs ~100B parameters in this recipe (later softened by T0 and FLAN-T5), the headline rides dev-selected best templates, and "instructions" here means academic NLP tasks rather than open-ended user requests. A foundational proof-of-concept, correctly hedged by its own authors, that launched the instruction-tuning era.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Instruction tuning, defined. Finetune a pretrained LM on many existing NLP datasets rephrased as natural-language instructions, and it converts from a few-shot-only model into one that follows zero-shot instructions for task types it never saw. FLAN 137B (instruction-tuned LaMDA-PT) beat zero-shot GPT-3 175B on 20 of 25 datasets.
  2. It emerges only at scale. Instruction tuning helps the 68B and 137B models but hurts held-out performance at ≤8B in this recipe — the paper's most-cited nuance. Later work (T0, FLAN-T5) showed the floor is recipe-dependent, not fundamental.
  3. The instructions are the mechanism, and the LaMDA-PT control is the proof. Same datasets with dataset-names instead of instructions score 47.0 vs FLAN's 55.2; no template at all, 37.3. And the credible evidence is the same-model ablation, not the cross-model GPT-3 headline.
